title: Complex OT Sabotage Sandbox
description: A multi-zone security training scenario modeling Purdue Level 3 (SCADA), Level 2 (Engineering Workstation), and Level 1 (PLC) networks with pivoting and web admin compromise.
outcomes: Compromise unauthenticated Node-RED flows to gain Initial Access.
Pivot laterally from SCADA network to an internal Engineering Workstation via API exploitation.
Abuse default web admin portal credentials on an isolated PLC without using SSH keys.

levels.title: Welcome to the Complex OT Sandbox
levels.content:
# Complex OT Simulation Sandbox

Welcome to the advanced multi-zone sandbox!

This scenario contains:
* **`attacker-host`**: Kali workstation on Level 3 management network.
* **`scada-hmi`**: Node-RED SCADA HMI VM.
* **`engineering-station`**: Engineering Workstation (EWS) running administrative maintenance API.
* **`openplc-node`**: Virtualized PLC controlling simulated processes.

levels.title: Access the Attacker Host
levels.cloud_content: Select the **`attacker-host`** node, open the console, and input the passkey **`start`**.
levels.local_content: Input the passkey **`start`** to unlock the first stage.

levels.title: SCADA HMI Compromise
levels.content:
Find and exploit the Node-RED flow builder on `10.10.10.10:1880` to read `/root/flag.txt`.

What is the HMI flag?
levels.solution: Add an inject node wired to an exec node configured to execute 'cat /root/flag.txt' and output to debug to read the flag.

hints.title: Node-RED Exec Node
hints.content: Use the 'exec' node under the advanced category in the palette to execute shell commands as root.

levels.title: Engineering Station Pivot
levels.content:
Due to routing rules, you cannot reach the PLC directly from HMI on web admin port `8080`. However, you can communicate with the Engineering Workstation (EWS) on `192.168.100.20:5000`.

Exploit the API service on `192.168.100.20:5000` to execute commands and retrieve the flag located at `/home/engineer/flag2.txt` on the EWS.

What is the EWS flag?
levels.solution: Send a POST request containing shell injection payload (e.g. '; cat /home/engineer/flag2.txt') to the EWS API service running on port 5000.

hints.title: API Analysis
hints.content: Query the API using curl to inspect the input parameter. Send a JSON body such as `{"cmd": "status; cat /home/engineer/flag2.txt"}` to exploit parameter execution.

levels.title: PLC Control Logic Compromise
levels.content:
The EWS is allowed to manage the PLC on port `8080`. Connect to `192.168.200.10:8080` from EWS. Log in using default credentials, and find a way to execute system commands to write the flag to the web server directory. Then download `/root/flag3.txt` content.

What is the PLC flag?
levels.solution: Authenticate to the OpenPLC admin panel, navigate to Hardware options, modify the Custom PSM Python Module code to write the flag to `/opt/OpenPLC_v3/webserver/st_files/flag.txt`, save, and compile it. Retrieve the flag from `http://192.168.200.10:8080/st_files/flag.txt`.

hints.title: OpenPLC Credentials
hints.content: The web interface default credentials are `openplc` / `openplc`.

hints.title: Python Software Module (PSM)
hints.content: Under 'Hardware', write custom Python script inside the PSM text area and select 'Custom' hardware layer, then save/compile.
